compliance_cache_used: sprint/compliance-cache/indiana.json (REUSED from state-income-tax sibling; minimum_wage block read; no new compliance data written — this page is essentially federal FLSA + Indiana IC 22-2 ops layer, all of which lives in source statutes rather than the cache)
high_concern_flags: prompt_correction:exempt_salary_threshold — prompt correctly flagged that the Biden-era DOL 2024 rule was vacated (Texas v. DOL 15 Nov 2024) and the operative threshold in Indiana is the pre-2024 figure of $684/wk ($35,568/yr). Page uses $35,568 throughout and explicitly explains the vacatur context in a pad-note. Confirm against the DOL WHD before any client classification decision; DOL replacement rulemaking is on the agenda but not yet final.

factual_uncertainty:medium — Ind. Code § 22-2-4-1 pay-frequency rule cited as 'bi-weekly OR semi-monthly for most employees' per compliance cache and prompt. The exact statutory enumeration of categories permitted to be paid monthly (elected officials, certain exempt employees by election) is paraphrased — verify the explicit list against current IC § 22-2-4 text before relying on a specific category exemption.

factual_uncertainty:low — Ind. Code § 22-2-5-2 liquidated-damages cap stated as 'up to double the unpaid amount' per primary IC text; the per-day add-on at 10% per day (where applicable to specific case types) was not enumerated to keep the answer-first paragraphs clean. The page captures the worst-case framing (double + attorney's fees) which is sufficient for the buyer-facing decision; a full DOL wage-claim filing should review the per-day mechanic against the most recent Indiana case law.

factual_uncertainty:low — Ind. Code § 20-33-3 minor-employee break rule cited as 'one break of at least 30 minutes (may be split into two breaks of at least 15 minutes each) for shifts of 6+ hours for under-18 employees'. Per prompt, this was flagged 'verify' — page uses the cited framing pending Steen's review against the current statute text. Verify the duration mechanic against IC § 20-33-3-34 before promoting to indexable.

factual_uncertainty:low — Indiana minimum wage statute coverage at IC § 22-2-2-3 states the Indiana minimum wage law applies to employers with 2+ employees AND not covered by FLSA; federal preempts otherwise. Almost all Indiana private-sector employers are FLSA-covered, so the federal $7.25 floor applies in practice. Page uses 'matches federal floor exactly' framing to capture the practical reality without misstating the statute coverage mechanic.

pullquote_note: Pullquote anonymised to 'Teamed Client Operations' per provenance rule. Subject: the 10-business-day final-pay window as the operational discipline point in an otherwise federal-floor wage-and-hour state. No <cite> tag used. Tom Price-Daniel tagline close stays as brand voice.
voice_notes: v2 editorial standard applied: 4-paragraph capsule (stake → size → ack → promise), no statute refs in body prose, statutes only in tables (Ind. Code §, 29 C.F.R., 29 U.S.C.)

Three named Indiana characters introduced and reused: Owen (Indianapolis SaaS engineer, federal-floor FLSA salaried exempt), Maya (Fort Wayne manufacturing ops, hourly OT + bi-weekly pay frequency), Reid (Bloomington research analyst, borderline exempt classification)
H2 ratio: 5 statements + 3 direct questions (38% question rate, within v2 target band)
UK English in body prose (whilst, behaviour, organised — used naturally)
No em-dashes in body prose (linter-verified — — entities used in links-rail, em-dashes scrubbed from prose)
Answer-first ≤30 words per section opening, lead with the number
Anonymised Teamed Client Operations pullquote (10-business-day final-pay clock as discipline point)
Single fixed rate / Zero FX / Crossover signal all present in body
Human-first signal: 'named country specialist', 'in-house payroll specialist', 'message the same person', 'no support tickets, no chatbot triage' present
One platform signal: 'one platform', 'contractor through EOR to entity, one timeline, one platform' present
End-of-page disclaimer aside present above .meta-footer, parchment styling per v2 spec

Distinctive Indiana angle: this is a textbook federal-floor state for wage-hour; whole page is essentially 'what FLSA says, applied in Indiana, with state-specific pay-frequency, final-pay, and wage-assignment rules layered on'. The page never pretends there's a state premium where there isn't.

Inline visual: pad-note explaining the Texas v. DOL vacatur context for the $684/wk threshold (so readers understand why $35,568 is the operative number in Indiana, not the higher Biden-rule numbers some out-of-state HR teams may still be citing)
